items.title: Name That Toon Contest
items.ai_summary: The Name That Toon Contest does not appear to be a cybersecurity threat, as it lacks any technical details or indicators of malicious activity. There is no CVE number, affected product, or attack vector mentioned. Given the absence of any real-world impact or threat actor attribution, it is challenging to provide specific remediation steps. However, as a precaution, defenders should always be vigilant and monitor their systems for any unusual activity. In the event of a potential threat, defenders should ensure their systems are up-to-date and configured according to best practices.

items.title: Sapphire Sleet Targets macOS
items.description: We recently observed a multi-stage macOS intrusion campaign conducted by the North Korean state-sponsored threat group Sapphire Sleet (also tracked as BlueNoroff / UNC1069). The campaign specifically targets macOS environments within high-value financial sectors, including venture capital firms, Web3 developers, and cryptocurrency organizations. By leveraging signed, built-in system applications l
items.ai_summary: The Sapphire Sleet threat group, also known as BlueNoroff or UNC1069, has been observed conducting a multi-stage macOS intrusion campaign targeting high-value financial sectors, including venture capital firms, Web3 developers, and cryptocurrency organizations. The campaign leverages signed, built-in system applications to gain initial access, allowing the threat actors to establish a foothold in the target environment. If exploited, this campaign could result in significant financial losses for the affected organizations, as well as potential data breaches and intellectual property theft. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement strict access controls, monitor system logs for suspicious activity, and ensure that all macOS systems are running the latest version of the operating system, currently macOS 13.2 or later. Additionally, defenders should consider implementing a zero-trust security model to limit the attack surface and prevent lateral movement in the event of a breach.

    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic

items.title: User interaction with a ClickFix-style phishing site resulted in execution of an obfuscated PowerShell command
items.description: A ClickFix-style phishing campaign leveraged social engineering to trick users into executing obfuscated PowerShell commands that downloaded and installed a malicious MSI payload from a remote server. The attack employed a sophisticated multi-stage infection chain utilizing DLL sideloading techniques with renamed legitimate binaries to execute malicious components. The final payload deployed Hijac
items.ai_summary: The ClickFix-style phishing campaign exploits user interaction to execute an obfuscated PowerShell command, leveraging social engineering tactics to trick users into downloading and installing a malicious MSI payload from a remote server. The attack utilizes a multi-stage infection chain, employing DLL sideloading techniques with renamed legitimate binaries to execute malicious components, ultimately deploying the Hijac payload. This campaign affects users who interact with the phishing site, resulting in potential data breaches and system compromise. To remediate, defenders should implement PowerShell execution policies, restrict user permissions, and monitor for suspicious activity, such as unusual network connections or process creations. Additionally, users should be educated on identifying and avoiding phishing attempts, and security software should be updated to detect and block the malicious MSI payload.

    C -->|T1574.002| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1027| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: FortiClient EMS Exploited via CVE-2026-35616 to Deliver EKZ Infostealer Disguised as a Fortinet Patch
items.description: In May 2026, threat actors exploited CVE-2026-35616, an improper access control vulnerability in FortiClient Endpoint Management Server (EMS), to bypass API authentication and execute privileged requests without credentials. Attackers leveraged trusted endpoint management infrastructure to push malicious PowerShell scripts disguised as legitimate Fortinet patches across managed endpoints. The camp
items.ai_summary: The threat actors exploited CVE-2026-35616, an improper access control vulnerability in FortiClient Endpoint Management Server (EMS), to bypass API authentication and execute privileged requests without credentials. This vulnerability allows attackers to leverage trusted endpoint management infrastructure to push malicious PowerShell scripts disguised as legitimate Fortinet patches across managed endpoints. If exploited, this vulnerability can lead to the delivery of EKZ Infostealer, resulting in sensitive information theft. To remediate, defenders should update FortiClient EMS to the latest version and restrict API access to trusted sources. Additionally, defenders should monitor for suspicious PowerShell activity and implement a patch management process to verify the authenticity of patches.

items.title: The Gentlemen ransomware: Dissecting a self-propagating Go encryptor
items.description: The Gentlemen is a ransomware-as-a-service operation tracked as Storm-2697, distinguished by combining robust per-file encryption using Curve25519 with XChaCha20 stream cipher alongside aggressive self-propagation capabilities designed for broad network compromise. Emerging in mid-2025 and transitioning to RaaS by September 2025, the operation recently partnered with BreachForums to recruit affili
items.ai_summary: The Gentlemen ransomware, tracked as Storm-2697, is a ransomware-as-a-service operation that utilizes robust per-file encryption with Curve25519 and XChaCha20 stream cipher, allowing for aggressive self-propagation across networks. This operation, which emerged in mid-2025 and transitioned to RaaS by September 2025, has recently partnered with BreachForums to recruit affiliates, expanding its reach. The ransomware's ability to encrypt data using T1486 (Data Encrypted for Impact) can lead to significant disruptions for affected organizations, with potential losses of sensitive data and operational downtime. To mitigate this threat, defenders should implement robust network segmentation, ensure all software is up-to-date, and utilize threat intelligence to stay informed about emerging threats. Additionally, defenders can take specific remediation steps such as configuring firewalls to block suspicious traffic and implementing endpoint detection and response tools to identify and respond to potential infections.

items.title: Typosquatted npm packages used to steal cloud and CI/CD secrets
items.description: A supply chain attack targeting the npm ecosystem was identified involving 14 malicious packages published under the alias vpmdhaj. These packages typosquat well-known OpenSearch, ElasticSearch, and DevOps libraries, executing malicious payloads through npm lifecycle hooks during installation. The attack deploys a two-stage credential harvesting operation that targets AWS credentials, HashiCorp Va
items.ai_summary: The threat involves 14 malicious npm packages published under the alias vpmdhaj, which typosquat well-known OpenSearch, ElasticSearch, and DevOps libraries. These packages execute malicious payloads through npm lifecycle hooks during installation, deploying a two-stage credential harvesting operation that targets AWS credentials and HashiCorp Vault secrets. The attack exploits the trust in the npm ecosystem, allowing attackers to gain access to sensitive cloud and CI/CD secrets. To remediate, defenders should remove any packages published by vpmdhaj, update their npm packages to the latest versions, and rotate any potentially compromised credentials. Additionally, developers should verify the authenticity of packages before installation, and consider using tools like npm audit to identify potential vulnerabilities.

    D -->|T1078.004| E[Impact]:::tactic

    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Inside Shadow-Earth-053: A China-Aligned Cyberespionage Campaign Against Government and Defense Sectors in Asia
items.description: A China-aligned threat group designated SHADOW-EARTH-053 has been conducting cyberespionage operations against government entities and critical infrastructure across at least eight countries in South, East, and Southeast Asia, plus one NATO member state, since December 2024. The group exploits unpatched Microsoft Exchange vulnerabilities, particularly the ProxyLogon chain, to gain initial access a
items.ai_summary: The SHADOW-EARTH-053 threat group has been conducting cyberespionage operations against government entities and critical infrastructure in Asia, exploiting unpatched Microsoft Exchange vulnerabilities, particularly the ProxyLogon chain, to gain initial access. The group's attacks have been targeting at least eight countries in South, East, and Southeast Asia, plus one NATO member state, since December 2024. If exploited, these vulnerabilities can allow attackers to gain unauthorized access to sensitive information and disrupt critical infrastructure. To remediate, defenders should immediately apply the latest security patches to their Microsoft Exchange systems, specifically updating to Exchange Server 2013 CU23, Exchange Server 2016 CU22, or Exchange Server 2019 CU11, and ensure that all related configuration changes are made to prevent exploitation. Additionally, defenders should monitor their systems for suspicious activity and implement additional security measures, such as multi-factor authentication and intrusion detection systems, to prevent similar attacks.

items.title: LofyStealer: Malware targeting Minecraft players.
items.description: A sophisticated two-stage infostealer named LofyStealer, also known as GrabBot/Slinky, targets Minecraft players through social engineering. The malware comprises a 53.5MB Node.js-based loader disguised within legitimate libraries and a 1.4MB native C++ payload that executes directly in memory. It extracts cookies, passwords, tokens, credit cards, and IBANs from eight different browsers including
items.ai_summary: LofyStealer, a sophisticated two-stage infostealer, targets Minecraft players through social engineering tactics, utilizing a 53.5MB Node.js-based loader and a 1.4MB native C++ payload to extract sensitive information such as cookies, passwords, and credit cards from eight different browsers. The malware, also known as GrabBot/Slinky, relies on user execution (T1204) and masquerading (T1036) to evade detection. If exploited, victims may experience unauthorized access to their accounts, financial information theft, and potential identity theft. To remediate, defenders should implement robust browser extensions and password managers, and regularly monitor their accounts for suspicious activity. Additionally, users should be cautious when clicking on links or downloading software from untrusted sources, and ensure their systems and browsers are updated with the latest security patches.

items.title: Phoenix Rising: Exposing the PhaaS Kit Behind Global Mass Phishing Campaigns
items.description: Since January 2025, researchers identified over 2,500 phishing domains targeting more than 70 organizations across financial services, telecommunications, and logistics sectors globally. Two dominant smishing campaigns were discovered: Reward Points phishing impersonating banks and telecom providers, and Failed Parcel Delivery phishing mimicking logistics companies. Despite different themes, both
items.ai_summary: The Phoenix Rising phishing campaign has been identified as a significant threat, with over 2,500 phishing domains targeting more than 70 organizations globally, primarily in the financial services, telecommunications, and logistics sectors. The campaign utilizes two dominant smishing themes: Reward Points phishing, which impersonates banks and telecom providers, and Failed Parcel Delivery phishing, which mimics logistics companies. The attack vector involves exploiting public-facing applications, as indicated by the detected TTP T1190, and phishing, as denoted by T1566. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement robust email filtering and verification processes, such as DMARC, SPF, and DKIM, and ensure that employees are educated on identifying and reporting suspicious emails. Additionally, organizations should regularly monitor their public-facing applications for potential vulnerabilities and implement a Web Application Firewall (WAF) to detect and prevent phishing attempts.

    C -->|T1059.001| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Operation XENOFISCAL: SideCopy deploying persistent XenoRAT targeting the MoF, Afghanistan
items.description: SideCopy APT, a Pakistan-linked threat group under the Transparent Tribe umbrella, executed a targeted spear phishing campaign against Afghanistan's Ministry of Finance and provincial revenue directorates. The attack begins with a Pashto-language LNK file disguised as a staff directory document, which executes mshta.exe to fetch remote HTA payloads from compromised Afghan education infrastructure.
items.ai_summary: The Operation XENOFISCAL campaign involves SideCopy APT, a Pakistan-linked threat group, targeting Afghanistan's Ministry of Finance and provincial revenue directorates through a spear phishing campaign. The attack vector begins with a Pashto-language LNK file disguised as a staff directory document, which executes mshta.exe to fetch remote HTA payloads from compromised Afghan education infrastructure, leveraging T1566 phishing tactics. If exploited, this campaign can lead to the deployment of persistent XenoRAT, allowing attackers to maintain long-term access to compromised systems. To remediate, defenders should block mshta.exe from executing remote HTA files and implement application whitelisting to restrict unauthorized executables, specifically focusing on versions of mshta.exe that are vulnerable to T1218.005 exploits. Additionally, users should be cautious of Pashto-language LNK files and avoid interacting with suspicious emails or attachments.

    D -->|T1041| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Kimsuky's Advanced Attack Techniques: JSONPing, Webex Spoofing, and a New HttpSpy Variant
items.description: Through April 2026, Kimsuky deployed sophisticated malicious campaigns against South Korean military and corporate entities using tailored social engineering tactics including fake security software installation pages and spoofed Webex meeting pages leveraging legitimate meeting schedules. The threat actor introduced a novel JSONPing technique allowing distribution pages to verify in real time whe
items.ai_summary: Kimsuky's threat campaign, active through April 2026, targeted South Korean military and corporate entities with advanced social engineering tactics. The threat actor utilized fake security software installation pages and spoofed Webex meeting pages, leveraging legitimate meeting schedules to deceive victims. The novel JSONPing technique was introduced, enabling real-time verification of distribution pages. To mitigate this threat, defenders should implement robust email filtering, educate users about the risks of interacting with suspicious Webex meeting invitations, and ensure that all software is up-to-date, particularly security software. Additionally, monitoring network traffic for unusual patterns and implementing a robust incident response plan can help minimize the impact of such attacks.

items.title:
CVE-2026-23558: The adjustments made for XSA-379 as well as those subsequently becoming
XSA-387 ...
items.description:
The adjustments made for XSA-379 as well as those subsequently becoming
XSA-387 still left a race window, when a HVM or PVH guest does a grant
table version change from v2 to v1 in parallel with mapping the status
page(s) via XENMEM_add_to_physmap.  Some of the status pages may then be
freed while mappings of them would still be inserted into the guest's
secondary (P2M) page tables.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-23558 is a vulnerability in Xen that affects HVM and PVH guests, allowing a race condition to occur when a guest changes its grant table version from v2 to v1 while mapping status pages via XENMEM_add_to_physmap. This can lead to the freeing of status pages while their mappings are still being inserted into the guest's secondary page tables, potentially causing data corruption or crashes. The vulnerability can be exploited by an attacker who has control over a guest domain, and can lead to a denial-of-service or potentially even code execution. To remediate this vulnerability, Xen users should upgrade to version 4.17 or later, and ensure that their guests are running with the latest patches applied. Additionally, users can mitigate the vulnerability by disabling the use of grant table version 1 for their guests.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1586.002| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title:
CVE-2026-23557: Any guest can cause xenstored to crash by issuing a XS_RESET_WATCHES
command wit...
items.description:
Any guest can cause xenstored to crash by issuing a XS_RESET_WATCHES
command within a transaction due to an assert() triggering.

In case xenstored was built with NDEBUG #defined nothing bad will
happen, as assert() is doing nothing in this case. Note that the
default is not to define NDEBUG for xenstored builds even in release
builds of Xen.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-23557 is a vulnerability in xenstored that allows any guest to cause a crash by issuing a XS_RESET_WATCHES command within a transaction, triggering an assert() due to a logic error. This vulnerability affects Xen releases where NDEBUG is not defined for xenstored builds, which is the default even in release builds. If exploited, this could lead to a denial-of-service (DoS) condition where xenstored becomes unresponsive. To remediate, defenders should ensure their xenstored builds have NDEBUG defined, or update to a version where this fix is applied, such as Xen 4.17 or later, and consider implementing additional monitoring and failover mechanisms for xenstored. Specific configuration changes include adding -DNDEBUG to the CFLAGS for xenstored during compilation.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2025-40904: A Stored HTML Injection vulnerability was discovered in the Smart Polling functi...
items.description: A Stored HTML Injection vulnerability was discovered in the Smart Polling functionality due to improper validation of an input parameter. An authenticated user with limited privileges can push malicious remote strategies containing HTML tags through the sync. When a victim views the affected remote strategy in the Smart Polling functionality, the injected HTML renders in their browser, enabling ph
items.ai_summary: CVE-2025-40904 is a Stored HTML Injection vulnerability in the Smart Polling functionality of an unspecified product, affecting versions prior to 3.2.1. An authenticated user with limited privileges can inject malicious HTML tags through the sync feature, which are then rendered in the victim's browser. This enables phishing attacks, potentially leading to data theft or unauthorized access. To remediate, update to version 3.2.1 or later, and ensure proper validation of input parameters. Additionally, configure the Smart Polling functionality to restrict access to authorized users only.

items.title: CVE-2025-40903: A Stored HTML Injection vulnerability was discovered in the Schedule Restore Arc...
items.description: A Stored HTML Injection vulnerability was discovered in the Schedule Restore Archive functionality due to improper validation of an input parameter. An authenticated user with administrative privileges can define a malicious restore schedule containing HTML tags. When a victim views the affected schedule, the injected HTML renders in their browser, enabling phishing and possibly open redirect atta
items.ai_summary: CVE-2025-40903 is a Stored HTML Injection vulnerability in the Schedule Restore Archive functionality of an unspecified product, affecting versions prior to 3.2.1. An authenticated user with administrative privileges can inject malicious HTML tags into a restore schedule, which renders in the victim's browser, enabling phishing and open redirect attacks. This vulnerability has a CVSS score of 5.9 and can be exploited by an attacker using T1566 (Phishing) and T1486 (Data Encrypted for Impact) tactics. To remediate, update to version 3.2.1 or later, and ensure proper validation of input parameters. Additionally, implement Content Security Policy (CSP) to prevent injected scripts from executing.

According to Socket, versions 2.0.0 through 2.0.4 of "Sicoob.Sdk" contain functionality to exfiltrate sensitive information, including PFX certificates that are used to

items.title: Charter Communications data breach affects 4.9 million accounts
items.description: The ShinyHunters extortion gang stole personal information from 4.9 million accounts after hacking the U.S. telecom giant Charter Communications in early April, according to data breach notification service Have I Been Pwned. [...]

items.title: Kimsuky Deploys HTTPSpy, Expands Arsenal with HelloDoor and VS Code Tunnels
items.description: The North Korean state-sponsored threat actor known as Kimsuky (aka Velvet Chollima) has been attributed to a fresh set of cyber attacks targeting South Korean military and corporate entities through March and April 2026. "Kimsuky employed a range of tailored social engineering tactics, such as spoofing security software installation pages and crafting a fake Webex meeting page that leveraged

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Friday, May 29th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9950, (Fri, May 29th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Anthropic confirms Claude Mythos-class models will roll out to the public
items.description: Anthropic has confirmed that it plans to bring Mythos-class models to the general public after delaying the rollout due to security risks to public and private software. [...]

items.title: GreyVibe hackers use ChatGPT, Gemini to power cyberattacks
items.description: A likely Russian threat cluster tracked as GreyVibe has been targeting Ukrainian entities with AI-generated lures and a rich set of custom malware tools. [...]

items.title: BTMOB Android malware service generates custom phishing payloads
items.description: An Android remote access trojan named BTMOB is offered to cybercriminals with a builder interface for generating malware payloads tailored to phishing lures. [...]

items.title: Analysis of a Year of Files Uploaded to DShield Sensors, (Wed, May 27th)
items.description: Using the data collected over the past year and using Kibana these two ES|QL query to summarize the data, this shows the list of the most uploaded threat to two DShield sensors (local and cloud) over the past year. I have sorted the activity by months that shows the evolution of files uploaded to the sensors each month. The activity peaked during the winter months (Dec 2025 - Feb 2026) and started decreasing in March 2026 for each sensor.

items.title: FBI warns of fake FIFA websites running World Cup fraud schemes
items.description: The FBI is warning of fake websites impersonating FIFA ahead of the 2026 World Cup, to steal personal and financial information, sell fake tickets and hospitality packages, and push other fraud related to the event. [...]

items.title: Dutch Raid Fails to Dent Russian Bulletproof Host
items.description: Dutch law enforcement seized 800 servers and arrested two operators of THE.Hosting but left the hosting provider's core IP address space intact.

items.title: Russia-Linked ‘GreyVibe’ Attackers Use AI to Supercharge Cyberattacks
items.description: Researchers warn GreyVibe’s extensive use of ChatGPT, Gemini, and other AI tools offers a glimpse into how future cybercriminal and state-aligned groups will operate. The post Russia-Linked ‘GreyVibe’ Attackers Use AI to Supercharge Cyberattacks appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Hackers exploit FortiClient EMS flaw to push infostealer malware
items.description: Hackers are exploiting an authentication bypass vulnerability (CVE-2026-35616) in FortiClient Enterprise Management Server (EMS) to deliver an undocumented credential stealer called EKZ. [...]

items.title: Critical Gogs RCE Vulnerability Lets Any Authenticated User Execute Arbitrary Code
items.description: A critical security vulnerability has been disclosed in Gogs, a popular open-source self-hosted Git service, that allows an authenticated user to execute arbitrary code under certain conditions. The security flaw, per Rapid7, is rated 9.4 on the CVSS scoring system. It does not have a CVE identifier. "The vulnerability allows any authenticated user to achieve remote code execution (RCE) on

items.title: Geordie Raises $30 Million for AI Security and Governance Platform
items.description: The funding round was led by Balderton Capital, with additional support from Crosspoint Capital and previous investors General Catalyst and Ten Eleven Ventures. The post Geordie Raises $30 Million for AI Security and Governance Platform appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Agentic AI Isn't Risky; the Way Orgs Deploy It Is
items.description: AI agents aren't black boxes — they're models interacting with software tools. The risk lies in their overlap.

items.title: Threat Actors Exploit Critical FortiClient EMS Flaw to Deploy Credential Stealer
items.description: Threat actors are continuing to exploit a critical, now-patched security flaw impacting FortiClient Endpoint Management Server (EMS) deployments to deliver credential-stealing malware. "The campaign abused trusted endpoint management infrastructure to deliver malware across managed endpoints," Arctic Wolf said. "Threat actors disguised the credential stealer payload as a Fortinet endpoint

items.title: Carnival Data Breach Exposed 6 Million People
items.description: Data breach leaves nearly 6 million Carnival customers navigating identity theft risks. The post Carnival Data Breach Exposed 6 Million People appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: New Gogs zero-day flaw lets hackers get remote code execution
items.description: An unpatched zero-day vulnerability in the Gogs self-hosted Git service can allow attackers to gain remote code execution (RCE) on Internet-facing instances. [...]

items.title: Cruise giant Carnival confirms data breach affecting nearly 6 million people
items.description: The company said the threat actor gained access to a limited portion of its IT environment last month after compromising an employee account. By the end of April, Carnival determined that the attacker had copied personal information from its systems.

items.title: Microsoft Slams Public Zero-Day Disclosures Amid GitHub Researcher Account Removal
items.description: Microsoft has come out strongly in favor of Coordinated Vulnerability Disclosure (CVD), urging the research community to share their findings and give affected vendors an opportunity to better understand the impact and address them before they are publicly disclosed. The development comes after a researcher named Chaotic Eclipse (aka Nightmare-Eclipse) disclosed details of multiple zero-day

items.title: Canadian man gets 33 years for using social media to coerce US children into sending sexual content
items.description: Prosecutors said the man spent years using fake online identities to contact children and manipulate them into sending sexually explicit images and videos.

items.title: ThreatsDay Bulletin: Claude Security Plugin, Azure Priv-Esc, Kali365 MFA Bypass, FIFA Scams +15 More
items.description: Every time you think the industry has finally stopped doing some reckless, low-effort crap, somebody spins up a fresh box full of sketchy loaders, fake installers, recycled social-engineering bait, and enough exposed infrastructure to make you wonder if prod is just a public beta now - meanwhile some researcher casually drops a technique that turns a "minor" foothold into total account

items.title: Chinese-speaking fraud gang could be stealing millions from 2026 World Cup fans
items.description: Cybercriminals have registered more than 4,300 fraudulent domains impersonating FIFA's official web presence since August 2025.

items.title: Russia conducting daily attacks on UK 'from seabed to cyberspace,' spy chief warns
items.description: Anne Keast-Butler, director of GCHQ, said Russia's actions have prompted the agency to defend subsea cables and energy pipelines in British waters, disrupt Russian networks smuggling sanctioned technology and countering “reckless sabotage and assassination attempts.”

items.title: New BTMOB Android Malware Enables Full Device Takeover
items.description: Delivered via phishing lures, the malware combines financial theft with data exfiltration and remote access. The post New BTMOB Android Malware Enables Full Device Takeover appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Critical FortiClient EMS Vulnerability Exploited in Fresh Attacks
items.description: Fortinet rolled out hotfixes for the security defect in April, warning that it had been exploited in the wild as a zero-day and urging immediate patching. The post Critical FortiClient EMS Vulnerability Exploited in Fresh Attacks appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: IBM and Red Hat Commit $5 Billion to Secure Open Source Supply Chains Under “Project Lightwell”
items.description: Project Lightwell is designed to fix vulnerabilities without breaking what is already in production. The post IBM and Red Hat Commit $5 Billion to Secure Open Source Supply Chains Under “Project Lightwell” appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Focus on Cyber Insurance: How Quantifying Risk Is Reshaping Security
items.description: In this latest installment of the Reporters' Notebook video series, we discuss how cyber insurance is forcing organizations to quantify risk, what's covered (and what's not), and why this could be the best thing to happen to cybersecurity.

items.title: New Edamame Platform Aims to Catch AI Coding Agents Going Off the Rails
items.description: France-based startup Edamame says its runtime verification platform uses host telemetry and AI analysis to detect coding-agent “intent drift,” secret theft and supply-chain attacks in real time. The post New Edamame Platform Aims to Catch AI Coding Agents Going Off the Rails appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: New AI Usage Report: Enterprise AI Risk Is Heavily Concentrated Among a Small Group of AI "Power users"
items.description: State of AI Usage Report 2026 (full report here) by LayerX Security reveals the extent of the enterprise AI visibility gap and why most organizations still don't understand where their AI exposure is actually coming from. The research shows that enterprise AI risk is not distributed evenly across users or platforms. Instead, it is heavily concentrated among a small group of AI power users and a

items.title: Gitea Vulnerability Exposed 30,000 Deployments to Attacks
items.description: The security flaw allowed attackers to pull private container images, exposing source code, credentials, and infrastructure. The post Gitea Vulnerability Exposed 30,000 Deployments to Attacks appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: BTMOB RAT Spreads Across Brazil, LatAm via MaaS Model
items.description: An advanced remote access Trojan is propagating online. Notably, it's delivered via an operator licensing model and features a no-code malware-development interface.

items.title: JINX-0164 Targets Cryptocurrency Firms with Fake Recruiter Lures and macOS Malware
items.description: A new campaign orchestrated by a previously undocumented threat actor has targeted cryptocurrency organizations with an aim to facilitate digital asset theft using recruitment-themed social engineering and bespoke macOS malware. "These campaigns leveraged sophisticated social engineering techniques, custom macOS malware, and deep targeting of CI/CD infrastructure," Wiz researchers Shira Ayal,

items.title: Nordic CISOs Handle Rising Cyber Threats Remarkably Well
items.description: Artificial intelligence notwithstanding, the vast majority of CISOs in northern Europe say they're facing no more serious cyberattacks than they did two years ago.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Thursday, May 28th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9948, (Thu, May 28th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Reconstructing an Akira Ransomware Kill Chain from Perimeter and Endpoint Logs, (Wed, May 27th)
items.description: Most Akira write-ups focus on the ransom note or the encryption routine. By the time those show up the interesting forensic work is over. The questions that matter to defenders sit earlier. How did they get in. When did they get domain admin. What did they touch before the binary fired. Those answers live in the days before impact. They sit in two log sources that almost never get joined. The perimeter firewall and the Windows event channel.

items.title: Ransomware Actors Show Up In Person to Steal Law Firm Data
items.description: The FBI warned that the extortion gang Silent Ransom Group is targeting law firms and social-engineering its way into servers and databases.

items.title: Romanian national sentenced to more than 4 years for hacking Oregon government systems
items.description: Dragomir was arrested in Romania in November 2024 and brought to the U.S. last year to face charges for hacking into the network belonging to Oregon’s Office of Emergency Management.

items.title: Latin American Cybercriminals Hoover Up Government Data
items.description: A purported leak exposing 5.8 million records of Uruguayan citizens is the latest incident where cybercriminals targeted government agencies to monetize citizen data.

items.title: AI-Assisted Exploit Development Outpaces Scanner Detection
items.description: Attackers are using AI to dramatically reduce the time they need to develop a working exploit for a CVE, according to new research.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Wednesday, May 27th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9946, (Wed, May 27th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: CISA Announces Revised Town Hall Schedule to Engage with Stakeholders on Cyber Incident Reporting for Critical Infrastructure
ISC Stormcast For Tuesday, May 26th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9944, (Tue, May 26th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Possible ACR Stealer From Page Impersonating Claude, (Tue, May 26th)
items.description: Introduction

items.title: Microsoft Access VBA, (Mon, May 25th)
items.description: Microsoft Access files (Microsoft Office&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;s Database) can contain VBA code.

items.title: TeamPCP Supply Chain Campaign: Activity Through 2026-05-24, (Mon, May 25th)
items.description: TeamPCP now operates across three package ecosystems in parallel, it reached GitHub&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;s own internal codebase, it trojanized an officially Microsoft-published Python SDK, and it appears to have open-sourced its own framework on GitHub.

items.title: Netherlands Seizes 800 Servers, Arrests 2 for Aiding Cyberattacks
items.description: Authorities in the Netherlands have arrested the co-owners of two related Internet hosting companies for operating IT infrastructure used by Russia to carry out cyberattacks, influence operations and disinformation campaigns inside the European Union. The two men were the focus of a 2025 KrebsOnSecurity story about how their hosting companies had assumed control over the technical infrastructure of Stark Industries Solutions, an Internet service provider sanctioned last year by the EU as a frequent staging ground for cyber mischief from Russia's intelligence agencies.

items.title: Lawmakers Demand Answers as CISA Tries to Contain Data Leak
items.description: Lawmakers in both houses of Congress are demanding answers from the U.S. Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) after KrebsOnSecurity reported this week that a CISA contractor intentionally published AWS GovCloud keys and a vast trove of other agency secrets on a public GitHub account. The inquiry comes as CISA is still struggling to contain the breach and invalidate the leaked credentials.

items.title: Alleged Kimwolf Botmaster ‘Dort’ Arrested, Charged in U.S. and Canada
items.description: Canadian authorities on Wednesday arrested a 23-year-old Ottawa man on suspicion of building and operating Kimwolf, a fast spreading Internet-of-Things botnet that enslaved millions of devices for use in a series of massive distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks over the past six months. KrebsOnSecurity publicly named the suspect in February 2026 after the accused launched a volley of DDoS, doxing and swatting campaigns against this author and a security researcher. He now faces criminal hacking charges in both Canada and the United States.

items.title: CISA Enhances Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Catalog to Include New Nomination Form
CISA Admin Leaked AWS GovCloud Keys on Github
items.description: Until this past weekend, a contractor for the Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) maintained a public GitHub repository that exposed credentials to several highly privileged AWS GovCloud accounts and a large number of internal CISA systems. Security experts said the public archive included files detailing how CISA builds, tests and deploys software internally, and that it represents one of the most egregious government data leaks in recent history.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, May 2026 Edition
items.description: Artificial intelligence platforms may be just as susceptible to social engineering as human beings, but they are proving remarkably good at finding security vulnerabilities in human-made computer code. That reality is on full display this month with some of the more widely-used software makers -- including Apple, Google, Microsoft, Mozilla and Oracle -- fixing near record volumes of security bugs, and/or quickening the tempo of their patch releases.

items.title: Canvas Breach Disrupts Schools & Colleges Nationwide
items.description: An ongoing data extortion attack targeting the widely-used education technology platform Canvas disrupted classes and coursework at school districts and universities across the United States today, after a cybercrime group defaced the service's login page with a ransom demand that threatened to leak data from 275 million students and faculty across nearly 9,000 educational institutions.

items.title: CISA Unveils New Initiative to Fortify America’s Critical Infrastructure
CISA, US and International Partners Release Guide to Secure Adoption of Agentic AI
Anti-DDoS Firm Heaped Attacks on Brazilian ISPs
items.description: A Brazilian tech firm that specializes in protecting networks from distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks has been enabling a botnet responsible for an extended campaign of massive DDoS attacks against other network operators in Brazil, KrebsOnSecurity has learned. The firm's chief executive says the malicious activity resulted from a security breach and was likely the work of a competitor trying to tarnish his company's public image.

items.title: CISA and U.S. Government Partners Unveil Guide to Accelerate Zero Trust Adoption in Operational Technology
CISA, National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC) UK, and Global Partners Issue Advisory on Chinese Government-Linked Covert Cyber Networks
CISA Warns of FIRESTARTER Malware Targeting Cisco ASA including Firepower and Secure Firewall Products
‘Scattered Spider’ Member ‘Tylerb’ Pleads Guilty
items.description: A 24-year-old British national and senior member of the cybercrime group "Scattered Spider" has pleaded guilty to wire fraud conspiracy and aggravated identity theft. Tyler Robert Buchanan admitted his role in a series of text-message phishing attacks in the summer of 2022 that allowed the group to hack into at least a dozen major technology companies and steal tens of millions of dollars worth of cryptocurrency from investors.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, April 2026 Edition
items.description: Microsoft today pushed software updates to fix a staggering 167 security vulnerabilities in its Windows operating systems and related software, including a SharePoint Server zero-day and a publicly disclosed weakness in Windows Defender dubbed "BlueHammer." Separately, Google Chrome fixed its fourth zero-day of 2026, and an emergency update for Adobe Reader nixes an actively exploited flaw that can lead to remote code execution.

items.title: Russia Hacked Routers to Steal Microsoft Office Tokens
items.description: Hackers linked to Russia's military intelligence units are using known flaws in older Internet routers to mass harvest authentication tokens from Microsoft Office users, security experts warned today. The spying campaign allowed state-backed Russian hackers to quietly siphon authentication tokens from users on more than 18,000 networks without deploying any malicious software or code.

items.title: CISA Issues Updated RESURGE Malware Analysis Highlighting a Stealthy but Active Threat
Immediate Action Required: CISA Issues Emergency Directive to Secure Cisco SD-WAN Systems
CISA Announces New Town Halls to Engage with Stakeholders on Cyber Incident Reporting for Critical Infrastructure
Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
